A GenAI Written Paper: A Central Element of 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12})

Jeffrey Kuan and Tailor Swift Bot

Abstract

The Type D asymmetric simple exclusion process (Type D ASEP) is a two-species interacting particle system exhibiting a drift, where two particles may occupy the same site only if they belong to different species. Previous work constructed the Type D ASEP from central elements of the quantum groups 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬6)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_6) and 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬8)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_8), while extended this to 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬10)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{10}). We compute an explicit central element of 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) and use it to construct the Type D ASEP with parameter n=6n = 6. The central element is obtained via the Lusztig bilinear pairing on the PBW basis, following the approach of . The key computational challenge is the evaluation of dual basis elements eμλ*e^*_{\mu\lambda} in two weight spaces of dimension 200 and 275; we describe an efficient pipeline using the bilinear pairing from and symbolic computation over ℚ(q)\mathbb{Q}(q). The D5D_5 subalgebra of D6D_6 allows 42 of the 65 central element terms to be transferred from the 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬10)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{10}) result by an index shift; the remaining 23 terms are computed directly.

Accessibility Statement

The webpage complies with Ohio Administrative Policy IT-09, Texas Administrative Code 206.70 (EFFECTIVE APRIL 18, 2020), Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act and Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act (effective April 24, 2026). This paper can also be downloaded as a DOCX file, and the TeX source code is available on the Ximera page. For the technical details related to assistive technology usage, please visit my accessibility guidelines. For any issues, please send me an email.

Introduction

The Type D asymmetric simple exclusion process (Type D ASEP) is an interacting particle system introduced in which generalizes the asymmetric simple exclusion process originally introduced by Spitzer . The state space consists of two species of particles interacting on a one-dimensional lattice, where two particles may occupy the same site as long as they are of different species. The process has three parameters (q,n,δ)(q, n, \delta): the asymmetry parameter qq, a positive integer nn controlling the drift speed, and δ\delta specifying the interaction between species.

The Type D ASEP has an unusual feature: neither class of particle has priority to jump over the other, and both the orthogonal polynomial duality function and the reversible measures factor over the two classes of particles. However, the two classes of particles have nontrivial interaction with each other. This suggests that the Type D ASEP converges to two independent copies of the KPZ equation and has asymptotic fluctuations which are two independent products of the Tracy–Widom distribution .

The procedure connecting quantum groups to interacting particle systems and Markov duality is described in ; see also . Applying this procedure to the Type D quantum groups 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬2n)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{2n}) produces the Type D ASEP. The key input is an explicit central element of 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬2n)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{2n}), from which the quantum Hamiltonian generating the process is extracted.

Interestingly, the parameter nn is the rank of the type D Lie algebra, but appears in the drift speed, rather than in the state space like with the nn-species ASEP arising from the AnA_n Lie algebra. One can naturally conjecture that the same phenomenon occurs in the exceptional simple Lie algebras E6,E7,E8E_6,E_7,E_8. The type EnE_n simple Lie algebras are not part of an infinite family of Lie algebras, so it would be curious if there is a type E ASEP with a parameter nn that does not come from a simple Lie algebra. One of the purposes of this paper is to verify that D6D_6 is computationally obtainable, before moving on to E6E_6, which is much larger.

Previous work has carried out this program for small nn. The authors of construct central elements of 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬6)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_6) and 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬8)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_8), corresponding to n=3n = 3 and n=4n = 4, and conjecture that the construction generalizes to all nn. The case n=5n = 5 (𝒰q(𝔰𝔬10)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{10})) is established in , where the central element is computed with the aid of numerical evaluation at a specific value of qq.

Results of this paper

In this paper we extend the construction to 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}), corresponding to n=6n = 6. Our main result is an explicit central element of 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) (Theorem [thm:central-element]), using a different method than the n=5n=5 case. The method in this paper is more computationally efficient and can complete on a MacBook Air with an Apple M4 Chip, with 16GB of memory.

In general, the central element of 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬2n)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{2n}) given by Lemma [lem:central] has (2n2)−1+2n\binom{2n}{2} - 1 + 2n terms: one for each pair μ≥λ\mu \geq \lambda among the 2n2n weights of V(ω1)V(\omega_1), minus the excluded pair (Ln,−Ln)(L_n, -L_n) since Ln=−LnL_n = -L_n in the weight ordering. For 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬10)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{10}) this gives (102)−1+10=54\binom{10}{2} - 1 + 10 = 54 terms, and for 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) this gives (122)−1+12=77\binom{12}{2} - 1 + 12 = 77 terms (12 diagonal and 65 off-diagonal).

Since D5D_5 embeds as the sub-diagram {α2,…,α6}\{\alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_6\} of D6D_6, the 54 terms of the 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬10)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{10}) central element transfer to 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) by an index shift and rescaling. The remaining 77−54=2377 - 54 = 23 terms—all involving the weight L1L_1 or −L1-L_1—are new. This paper focuses on computing these 23 new terms; taken together with the transferred 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬10)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{10}) result from , they yield the complete central element of 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}).

Each off-diagonal term takes the form Dμ,λeμ,λ*K−μ−λfλ,μ*,D_{\mu,\lambda} \;\, e^*_{\mu,\lambda} \;\, K_{-\mu-\lambda} \;\, f^*_{\lambda,\mu}, where Dμ,λ∈ℚ(q)D_{\mu,\lambda} \in \mathbb{Q}(q) is an explicit scalar, eμ,λ*e^*_{\mu,\lambda} and fλ,μ*f^*_{\lambda,\mu} are dual basis elements in the quantized enveloping algebra computed via the bilinear pairing of , and qH−μ−λq^{H_{-\mu-\lambda}} is the appropriate Cartan element.

Computational approach

The computation of the dual elements eμ,λ*e^*_{\mu,\lambda} is the main challenge. Following , we use the Lusztig bilinear pairing ⟨⋅,⋅⟩\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle on 𝒰q(𝔟−)×𝒰q(𝔟+)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{b}^-) \times \mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{b}^+). Each dual element is expressed in the Poincaré–Birkhoff–Witt (PBW) basis of the relevant weight space of 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}).

As observed in , direct symbolic computation of the pairing matrices becomes intractable for large weight spaces. We address this in two ways:

  1. Transfer from 𝔰𝔬10\mathfrak{so}_{10}. The Dynkin diagram D5D_5 embeds naturally as the sub-diagram {α2,…,α6}\{\alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_6\} of D6D_6. This allows 42 of the 65 pair terms to be obtained from the 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬10)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{10}) central element by shifting indices and rescaling. The remaining 23 terms, all involving the weight L1L_1 or −L1-L_1, require fresh computation.

  2. Symbolic pipeline over ℚ(q)\mathbb{Q}(q). For the new terms, we compute the bilinear pairing matrix Mij=⟨Pi,ωτ(Pj)⟩M_{ij} = \langle P_i, \omega\tau(P_j) \rangle (where ω\omega is the Chevalley anti-involution and {Pk}\{P_k\} is the PBW basis), invert it over ℚ(q)\mathbb{Q}(q), and extract each eμ,λ*e^*_{\mu,\lambda} via representation coefficients on V(ω1)V(\omega_1). This approach works for all weight spaces, including the two largest: dimension 200 (γ=L1+L2\gamma = L_1 + L_2) and dimension 275 (γ=2L1\gamma = 2L_1).

For the smaller weight spaces (dimensions up to 75), the pairing matrices can alternatively be computed by numerical evaluation at ∼50\sim\!50 rational values of qq followed by rational interpolation . For the two largest weight spaces, the symbolic pipeline described above is essential (unless one has access to a computing cluster).

Outline

The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews the necessary background on quantum groups, the bilinear pairing, and the Type D ASEP. Section 3 states the main results: the central element of 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) and the Type D ASEP generator for n=6n = 6. Section 4 outlines the proofs, including the computational methodology for obtaining the dual basis elements.

GenAI Statement

The majority of this paper was written by Claude CLi, using Opus 4.6, during March 2026. The primary motivation for the first author is related to his advising of undergraduate research. More specifically, he wanted to know if a student could write an undergraduate–level paper with minimal mathematics knowledge. For a point of reference, the first author knows nothing about SAGE, despite it being used heavily in this paper. For all he knows, SAGE could be three cats in a trenchcoat, named Meredith, Benjamin, and Olivia.

The first author acknowledges that some of this work was done on devices belonging to Texas A&M University.

Conflict of Interest Disclosure.

The namesake of the second author, “Tailor Swift Bot,” is a company originally founded by the first author while at Texas A&M University. It is a bot that is “tailored” for your needs and is “swift” (that joke never gets old). Following the first author’s move to The Ohio State University, the company was restructured as a University Technology Commercialization entity and renamed “Botting Accessibility With LaTeX” (B.A.W.L.) at the university’s request, due to obvious copyright issues related to a famous singer–songwriter.

B.A.W.L. is a University Technology Commercialization Company (UTCC) as defined in Chapter 3335-13 of The Ohio State University’s bylaws and rules. The views and products of B.A.W.L. are those of the company and do not necessarily reflect the views of, nor are they endorsed or approved by, The Ohio State University.

Background

The Lie algebra 𝔰𝔬12\mathfrak{so}_{12}

The special orthogonal Lie algebra 𝔰𝔬2n\mathfrak{so}_{2n} is defined as 𝔰𝔬2n={(ABC−AT)|A,B,C∈Mn×n(ℂ),B=−BT,C=−CT}.\mathfrak{so}_{2n} = \Biggl\{\begin{pmatrix} A & B \\ C & -A^T \end{pmatrix} \Bigg|A,B,C \in M_{n\times n}(\mathbb{C}), B = -B^T, C = -C^T\Biggl\}. This Lie algebra has rank nn and Dynkin diagram DnD_n. In this paper we take n=6n = 6.

Let Ei,jE_{i,j} denote the matrix with 11 in entry (i,j)(i, j) and 00 elsewhere. Define matrices Ei,Fi,HiE_i, F_i, H_i for 1≤i≤61 \leq i \leq 6 as follows. For 1≤i≤51 \leq i \leq 5, Ei=Ei,i+1−E7+i,6+i,Fi=Ei+1,i−E6+i,7+i,Hi=Ei,i−Ei+1,i+1−E6+i,6+i+E7+i,7+i,\begin{align*} E_i &= E_{i, i+1} - E_{7+i, 6+i}, \\ F_i &= E_{i+1, i} - E_{6+i, 7+i}, \\ H_i &= E_{i,i} - E_{i+1, i+1} - E_{6+i,6+i} + E_{7+i,7+i}, \end{align*} and for i=6i = 6, E6=E5,12−E6,11,F6=E12,5−E11,6,H6=E5,5+E6,6−E11,11−E12,12.\begin{align*} E_6 &= E_{5, 12} - E_{6, 11}, \\ F_6 &= E_{12, 5} - E_{11, 6}, \\ H_6 &= E_{5,5} + E_{6,6} - E_{11,11} - E_{12,12}. \end{align*} These matrices generate the fundamental representation of 𝔰𝔬12\mathfrak{so}_{12} on ℂ12\mathbb{C}^{12}.

The Cartan matrix (aij)1≤i,j≤6(a_{ij})_{1 \leq i,j \leq 6} of type D6D_6 is given by aij={2i=j,−1|i−j|=1 and max⁡(i,j)≤5,−1{i,j}={4,6},0otherwise.a_{ij} = \begin{cases} 2 & i = j, \\ -1 & |i - j| = 1 \text{ and } \max(i,j) \leq 5, \\ -1 & \{i, j\} = \{4, 6\}, \\ 0 & \text{otherwise}. \end{cases} The corresponding Dynkin diagram is:

Let LiL_i denote the linear functional taking a matrix in 𝔰𝔬12\mathfrak{so}_{12} to its ii-th diagonal entry. The positive roots of 𝔰𝔬12\mathfrak{so}_{12} are {Li+Lj}1≤i<j≤6∪{Li−Lj}1≤i<j≤6\{L_i + L_j\}_{1 \leq i < j \leq 6} \cup \{L_i - L_j\}_{1 \leq i < j \leq 6}, giving 30 positive roots in total. The simple roots are αi=Li−Li+1(1≤i≤5),α6=L5+L6.\alpha_i = L_i - L_{i+1} \quad (1 \leq i \leq 5), \qquad \alpha_6 = L_5 + L_6. The weights of the fundamental representation V(ω1)=ℂ12V(\omega_1) = \mathbb{C}^{12} are ±L1,…,±L6\pm L_1, \ldots, \pm L_6, ordered as L1>L2>⋯>L6=−L6>−L5>⋯>−L1.L_1 > L_2 > \cdots > L_6 = -L_6 > -L_5 > \cdots > -L_1. Define weight vectors v1,…,v12v_1, \ldots, v_{12} with viv_i in the weight space of LiL_i for 1≤i≤61 \leq i \leq 6, and v7,…,v12v_{7}, \ldots, v_{12} in the weight spaces of −L6,…,−L1-L_6, \ldots, -L_1, respectively.

The quantum group 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12})

The Drinfeld–Jimbo quantum group 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) is the associative ℚ(q)\mathbb{Q}(q)-algebra generated by {Ei,Fi,Ki,Ki−1:1≤i≤6}\{E_i, F_i, K_i, K_i^{-1} : 1 \leq i \leq 6\}, where Ki=qHiK_i = q^{H_i}, subject to the following relations. For all 1≤i,j≤61 \leq i, j \leq 6: KiKi−1=Ki−1Ki=1,KiKj=KjKi,KiEj=qaijEjKi,KiFj=q−aijFjKi,[Ei,Fj]=δijKi−Ki−1q−q−1.\begin{gather} K_i K_i^{-1} = K_i^{-1} K_i = 1, \qquad K_i K_j = K_j K_i, \label{eq:KK} \\ K_i E_j = q^{a_{ij}} E_j K_i, \qquad K_i F_j = q^{-a_{ij}} F_j K_i, \label{eq:KEF} \\ [E_i, F_j] = \delta_{ij} \frac{K_i - K_i^{-1}}{q - q^{-1}}. \label{eq:EF} \end{gather} If aij=0a_{ij} = 0 (i.e., nodes ii and jj are not connected in the Dynkin diagram), then EiEj=EjEi,FiFj=FjFi.\begin{equation} \label{eq:commute} E_i E_j = E_j E_i, \qquad F_i F_j = F_j F_i. \end{equation} If aij=−1a_{ij} = -1 (i.e., nodes ii and jj are connected), the Serre relations hold: Ei2Ej−(q+q−1)EiEjEi+EjEi2=0,Fi2Fj−(q+q−1)FiFjFi+FjFi2=0.\begin{equation} \label{eq:Serre} E_i^2 E_j - (q + q^{-1}) E_i E_j E_i + E_j E_i^2 = 0, \qquad F_i^2 F_j - (q + q^{-1}) F_i F_j F_i + F_j F_i^2 = 0. \end{equation}

The algebra 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) is a Hopf algebra with coproduct Δ(Ei)=Ei⊗1+Ki⊗Ei,Δ(Fi)=1⊗Fi+Fi⊗Ki−1,Δ(Ki)=Ki⊗Ki.\begin{equation} \label{eq:coprod} \Delta(E_i) = E_i \otimes 1 + K_i \otimes E_i, \qquad \Delta(F_i) = 1 \otimes F_i + F_i \otimes K_i^{-1}, \qquad \Delta(K_i) = K_i \otimes K_i. \end{equation}

For a general root β=∑iniαi\beta = \sum_{i} n_i \alpha_i, define Kβ=∏iKiniK_\beta = \prod_i K_i^{n_i}. In particular, KLi±LjK_{L_i \pm L_j} and K−Li∓LjK_{-L_i \mp L_j} will appear in the central element formula.

There is a unique ℚ(q)\mathbb{Q}(q)-algebra automorphism ω:𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)→𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\omega \colon \mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) \to \mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) (the Chevalley involution) defined on generators by ω(Ei)=Fi,ω(Fi)=Ei,ω(Ki)=Ki−1.\begin{equation} \label{eq:omega} \omega(E_i) = F_i, \qquad \omega(F_i) = E_i, \qquad \omega(K_i) = K_i^{-1}. \end{equation} There is also a unique ℚ(q)\mathbb{Q}(q)-algebra antiautomorphism τ:𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)→𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\tau \colon \mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) \to \mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) defined by τ(Ei)=Ei,τ(Fi)=Fi,τ(Ki)=Ki,\begin{equation} \label{eq:tau} \tau(E_i) = E_i, \qquad \tau(F_i) = F_i, \qquad \tau(K_i) = K_i, \end{equation} with τ(xy)=τ(y)τ(x)\tau(xy) = \tau(y)\tau(x) for all x,y∈𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)x, y \in \mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}). In particular, if eμλ=Ei1⋯Eike_{\mu\lambda} = E_{i_1} \cdots E_{i_k} is a product of raising operators, then τ(eμλ)=Eik⋯Ei1\tau(e_{\mu\lambda}) = E_{i_k} \cdots E_{i_1} and ω(τ(eμλ))=Fik⋯Fi1\omega(\tau(e_{\mu\lambda})) = F_{i_k} \cdots F_{i_1}.

We follow the conventions of . In particular, KiEi=q2EiKiK_i E_i = q^2 E_i K_i and KiFi=q−2FiKiK_i F_i = q^{-2} F_i K_i, and the Serre relations use q+q−1q + q^{-1} rather than 1+q±21 + q^{\pm 2}.

The bilinear pairing

Following , there is a bilinear pairing ⟨⋅,⋅⟩:𝒰q(𝔟−)×𝒰q(𝔟+)→ℚ(q)\langle \cdot\,,\, \cdot \rangle \colon \mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{b}^-) \times \mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{b}^+) \to \mathbb{Q}(q), where 𝒰q(𝔟±)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{b}^\pm) denote the Borel subalgebras generated by {Fi,Ki±1}\{F_i, K_i^{\pm 1}\} and {Ei,Ki±1}\{E_i, K_i^{\pm 1}\}, respectively. On generators: ⟨Kα,Kβ⟩=q−(α,β),⟨Fi,Ej⟩=−δijq−q−1,\langle K_\alpha, K_\beta \rangle = q^{-(\alpha, \beta)}, \qquad \langle F_i, E_j \rangle = \frac{-\delta_{ij}}{q - q^{-1}}, and all other pairings between generators are zero. The pairing extends to products via the coproduct: ⟨y,xx′⟩=⟨Δ(y),x′⊗x⟩,⟨yy′,x⟩=⟨y⊗y′,Δ(x)⟩,\langle y, xx' \rangle = \langle \Delta(y), x' \otimes x \rangle, \qquad \langle yy', x \rangle = \langle y \otimes y', \Delta(x) \rangle, where ⟨x1⊗x2,y1⊗y2⟩=⟨x1,y1⟩⟨x2,y2⟩\langle x_1 \otimes x_2, y_1 \otimes y_2 \rangle = \langle x_1, y_1 \rangle \langle x_2, y_2 \rangle.

This pairing is used to compute dual basis elements in the PBW basis, as described in Section 4.

Central element formula

The following lemma, due to , provides an explicit formula for a central element of a quantum group in terms of the bilinear pairing.

For each weight μ\mu of the fundamental representation of a Lie algebra 𝔤\mathfrak{g}, let vμv_\mu be a vector in the weight space. Suppose qq is not a root of unity, and 2μ2\mu is always in the root lattice of 𝔤\mathfrak{g}. Let eμλe_{\mu\lambda} and fλμf_{\lambda\mu} be products of EiE_i’s and FiF_i’s in 𝒰q(𝔤)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{g}), respectively, such that eμλe_{\mu\lambda} sends vλv_\lambda to vμv_\mu and fλμf_{\lambda\mu} sends vμv_\mu to vλv_\lambda. If eμλ*e_{\mu\lambda}^* and fλμ*f_{\lambda\mu}^* are the corresponding dual elements under ⟨⋅,⋅⟩\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle, and ρ\rho is half the sum of the positive roots of 𝔤\mathfrak{g}, then ∑μ≥λq(μ−λ,μ)q−(2ρ,μ)eμλ*qH−μ−λfλμ*\begin{equation} \label{eq:central-element} \sum_{\mu\geq\lambda} q^{(\mu - \lambda, \mu)} q^{-(2\rho, \mu)} e_{\mu\lambda}^*\,q^{H_{-\mu-\lambda}}\,f_{\lambda\mu}^* \end{equation} is a central element of 𝒰q(𝔤)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{g}).

Since fλμ*=ω(τ(eμλ*))f_{\lambda\mu}^* = \omega(\tau(e_{\mu\lambda}^*)) (reverse the order of factors and swap Ei↔FiE_i \leftrightarrow F_i), the formula [eq:central-element] can be rewritten as ∑μ≥λq(μ−λ,μ)q−(2ρ,μ)eμλ*K−μ−λω(τ(eμλ*)),\begin{equation} \label{eq:central-tau} \sum_{\mu\geq\lambda} q^{(\mu - \lambda, \mu)} q^{-(2\rho, \mu)} e_{\mu\lambda}^*\,K_{-\mu-\lambda}\,\omega(\tau(e_{\mu\lambda}^*)), \end{equation} so it suffices to compute only the eμλ*e^*_{\mu\lambda} terms.

For 𝔤=𝔰𝔬12\mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{so}_{12}, the sum in [eq:central-element] ranges over all pairs μ≥λ\mu \geq \lambda among the weights ±L1,…,±L6\pm L_1, \ldots, \pm L_6 of V(ω1)V(\omega_1), giving 12 diagonal terms (μ=λ\mu = \lambda) and 65 pair terms (μ>λ\mu > \lambda). The half-sum of positive roots satisfies (2ρ,Li)=2(6−i)(2\rho, L_i) = 2(6 - i) for 1≤i≤61 \leq i \leq 6, and the scalar prefactors are computed in : q(μ−λ,μ)={q2λ=−μ,1λ=μ,qotherwise,q−(2ρ,μ)={q2i−12μ=Li,q12−2iμ=−Li.q^{(\mu - \lambda, \mu)} = \begin{cases} q^2 & \lambda = -\mu, \\ 1 & \lambda = \mu, \\ q & \text{otherwise,} \end{cases} \qquad q^{-(2\rho, \mu)} = \begin{cases} q^{2i - 12} & \mu = L_i, \\ q^{12 - 2i} & \mu = -L_i. \end{cases}

Results

The Dynkin diagram D5D_5 embeds as the sub-diagram {α2,…,α6}\{\alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_6\} of D6D_6. Let C10∈𝒰q(𝔰𝔬10)C_{10} \in \mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{10}) denote the central element constructed in , and let C̃10∈𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\widetilde{C}_{10} \in \mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) denote the image of C10C_{10} under the corresponding index shift αi↦αi+1\alpha_i \mapsto \alpha_{i+1} and rescaling. We use the shorthand Fi1i2⋯ikF_{i_1 i_2 \cdots i_k} for the root vectors, write Dμ,λ=q(μ−λ,μ)q−(2ρ,μ)D_{\mu,\lambda} = q^{(\mu-\lambda,\,\mu)}\,q^{-(2\rho,\,\mu)} for the scalar prefactor, and recall that fλμ*=ω(τ(eμλ*))f^*_{\lambda\mu} = \omega(\tau(e^*_{\mu\lambda})), obtained by reversing the order of factors and swapping Ei↔FiE_i \leftrightarrow F_i.

The element C12=C̃10+C̃12C_{12} = \widetilde{C}_{10} + \widetilde{C}_{12} is in the center of 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}), where C̃12\widetilde{C}_{12} consists of the following 23 new terms.

  1. The two Cartan terms of C̃12\widetilde{C}_{12} are q10K2L1+q−10K−2L1,q^{10}\, K_{2L_1} \;+\; q^{-10}\, K_{-2L_1}, and the 21 off-diagonal terms of C̃12\widetilde{C}_{12} each take the form Dμ,λeμ,λ*K−μ−λω(τ(eμ,λ*))D_{\mu,\lambda}\, e^*_{\mu,\lambda}\, K_{-\mu-\lambda}\, \omega(\tau(e^*_{\mu,\lambda})). The scalar prefactors Dμ,λD_{\mu,\lambda} and KK-labels are:

    Pair (μ,λ)(\mu,\lambda) Dμ,λD_{\mu,\lambda} K−μ−λK_{-\mu-\lambda} Pair (μ,λ)(\mu,\lambda) Dμ,λD_{\mu,\lambda} K−μ−λK_{-\mu-\lambda}

    The dual elements eμ,λ*e^*_{\mu,\lambda} are:

    e(L1,L2)*=((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E1e^*_{(L_1, L_2)} = ((-1) (q-1) (q+1))/(q)\, E_{1}

  2. The element C̃10\widetilde{C}_{10} consists of the 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬10)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{10}) central element embedded in 𝒰q(𝔰𝔬12)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{so}_{12}) via the sub-diagram {α2,…,α6}\{\alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_6\}, comprising 10 diagonal terms and 44 off-diagonal terms involving the weights ±L2,…,±L6\pm L_2, \ldots, \pm L_6.

    e(L4,−L4)*=((q−1)2(q+1)2(q2+1))/(q2)E4564+((−1)(q−1)2(q+1)2)/(q)E4645+((−1)(q−1)2(q+1)2)/(q)E5464+(q−1)2(q+1)2E5644+(q−1)2(q+1)2E6445+((−1)(q−1)2(q+1)2(q2+1))/(q)E6454\begin{multline*} e^*_{(L_4, -L_4)} = ((q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}(q^2+1))/(q^2)\,E_{4564} + ((-1)(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2})/(q)\,E_{4645} + ((-1)(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2})/(q)\,E_{5464} \\ + (q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}\,E_{5644} + (q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}\,E_{6445} + ((-1)(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}(q^2+1))/(q)\,E_{6454} \end{multline*}

    e(L4,−L3)*=(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E34564+(q−1)(q+1)q2E35464+(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E35644+(q−1)(q+1)q4E36454+(q−1)(q+1)q2E43456+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E43546+(q−1)(q+1)(q2+1)E43564+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E43645+(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E44356+(q−1)(q+1)q2E45436+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E45643+(q−1)(q+1)(q4−q2+1)E46435+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E54364+(−1)(q−1)2(q+1)2E54643+(q−1)(q+1)q2E56434+q(q−1)2(q+1)2E56443+(−1)(q−1)2q2(q+1)2E64345+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E64354+q(q−1)2(q+1)2E64435+(−1)(q−1)2(q+1)2(q2+1)E64543\begin{multline*} e^*_{(L_4, -L_3)} = (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{34564} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{35464} + (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{35644} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)q^4\,E_{36454} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{43456} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{43546} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)(q^2+1)\,E_{43564} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{43645} + (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{44356} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{45436} + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{45643} + (q-1)(q+1)(q^4 - q^2+1)\,E_{46435} \\ + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{54364} + (-1)(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}\,E_{54643} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{56434} \\ + q(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}\,E_{56443} + (-1)(q-1)^{2}q^2(q+1)^{2}\,E_{64345} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{64354} \\ + q(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}\,E_{64435} + (-1)(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}(q^2+1)\,E_{64543} \end{multline*}

    e(L4,−L2)*=(q−1)(q+1)q4E234564+(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E235464+(q−1)(q+1)q4E235644+(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q5E236454+(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E243456+(q−1)(q+1)q2E243546+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)(q2+1)E243564+(q−1)(q+1)q2E243645+(q−1)(q+1)q4E244356+(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E245436+(q−1)(q+1)E245643+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)(q4−q2+1)E246435+(q−1)(q+1)q2E254364+q(q−1)2(q+1)2E254643+(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E256434+(−1)(q−1)2q2(q+1)2E256443+(q−1)2(q+1)2q3E264345+(q−1)(q+1)q2E264354+(−1)(q−1)2q2(q+1)2E264435+q(q−1)2(q+1)2(q2+1)E264543+(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E324564+(q−1)(q+1)q2E325464+(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E325644+(q−1)(q+1)q4E326454+(q−1)(q+1)q2E432456+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E432546+(q−1)(q+1)(q2+1)E432564+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E432645+(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E443256+(q−1)(q+1)q2E454326+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E456432+(q−1)(q+1)(q4−q2+1)E464325+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E543264+(−1)(q−1)2(q+1)2E546432+(q−1)(q+1)q2E564324+q(q−1)2(q+1)2E564432+(−1)(q−1)2q2(q+1)2E643245+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E643254+q(q−1)2(q+1)2E644325+(−1)(q−1)2(q+1)2(q2+1)E645432\begin{multline*} e^*_{(L_4, -L_2)} = (q-1)(q+1)q^4\,E_{234564} + (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{235464} + (q-1)(q+1)q^4\,E_{235644} \\ + (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^5\,E_{236454} + (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{243456} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{243546} \\ + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)(q^2+1)\,E_{243564} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{243645} + (q-1)(q+1)q^4\,E_{244356} \\ + (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{245436} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{245643} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)(q^4 - q^2+1)\,E_{246435} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{254364} + q(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}\,E_{254643} + (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{256434} \\ + (-1)(q-1)^{2}q^2(q+1)^{2}\,E_{256443} + (q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}q^3\,E_{264345} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{264354} \\ + (-1)(q-1)^{2}q^2(q+1)^{2}\,E_{264435} + q(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}(q^2+1)\,E_{264543} + (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{324564} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{325464} + (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{325644} + (q-1)(q+1)q^4\,E_{326454} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{432456} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{432546} + (q-1)(q+1)(q^2+1)\,E_{432564} \\ + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{432645} + (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{443256} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{454326} \\ + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{456432} + (q-1)(q+1)(q^4 - q^2+1)\,E_{464325} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{543264} \\ + (-1)(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}\,E_{546432} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{564324} + q(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}\,E_{564432} \\ + (-1)(q-1)^{2}q^2(q+1)^{2}\,E_{643245} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{643254} + q(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}\,E_{644325} \\ + (-1)(q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2}(q^2+1)\,E_{645432} \end{multline*}

    e(L5,L6)*=((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E5e^*_{(L_5, L_6)} = ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{5}

    e(L5,−L6)*=((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E6e^*_{(L_5, -L_6)} = ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{6}

    e(L5,−L5)*=((q−1)2(q+1)2)/(q2)E56e^*_{(L_5, -L_5)} = ((q-1)^{2}(q+1)^{2})/(q^2)\,E_{56}

    e(L5,−L4)*=(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E456+(q−1)(q+1)E546+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E564+(q−1)(q+1)E645\begin{multline*} e^*_{(L_5, -L_4)} = (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{456} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{546} + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{564} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{645} \end{multline*}

    e(L5,−L3)*=(q−1)(q+1)q2E3456+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E3546+(q−1)(q+1)E3564+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E3645+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E4356+(q−1)(q+1)E5436+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E5643+(q−1)(q+1)E6435\begin{multline*} e^*_{(L_5, -L_3)} = (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{3456} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{3546} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{3564} \\ + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{3645} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{4356} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{5436} \\ + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{5643} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{6435} \end{multline*}

    e(L5,−L2)*=(−1)(q−1)(q+1)q3E23456+(q−1)(q+1)q2E23546+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E23564+(q−1)(q+1)q2E23645+(q−1)(q+1)q2E24356+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E25436+(q−1)(q+1)E25643+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E26435+(q−1)(q+1)q2E32456+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E32546+(q−1)(q+1)E32564+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E32645+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E43256+(q−1)(q+1)E54326+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E56432+(q−1)(q+1)E64325\begin{multline*} e^*_{(L_5, -L_2)} = (-1)(q-1)(q+1)q^3\,E_{23456} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{23546} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{23564} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{23645} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{24356} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{25436} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{25643} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{26435} + (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{32456} \\ + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{32546} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{32564} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{32645} \\ + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{43256} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{54326} + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{56432} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{64325} \end{multline*}

    e(L6,−L5)*=((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E6e^*_{(L_6, -L_5)} = ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{6}

    e(L6,−L4)*=(q−1)(q+1)E46+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E64e^*_{(L_6, -L_4)} = (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{46} + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{64}

    e(L6,−L3)*=(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E346+(q−1)(q+1)E364+(q−1)(q+1)E436+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E643\begin{multline*} e^*_{(L_6, -L_3)} = (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{346} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{364} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{436} \\ + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{643} \end{multline*}

    e(L6,−L2)*=(q−1)(q+1)q2E2346+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E2364+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E2436+(q−1)(q+1)E2643+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E3246+(q−1)(q+1)E3264+(q−1)(q+1)E4326+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E6432\begin{multline*} e^*_{(L_6, -L_2)} = (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{2346} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{2364} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{2436} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{2643} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{3246} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{3264} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{4326} + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{6432} \end{multline*}

    e(−L6,−L5)*=((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E5e^*_{(-L_6, -L_5)} = ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{5}

    e(−L6,−L4)*=(q−1)(q+1)E45+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E54e^*_{(-L_6, -L_4)} = (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{45} + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{54}

    e(−L6,−L3)*=(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E345+(q−1)(q+1)E354+(q−1)(q+1)E435+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E543\begin{multline*} e^*_{(-L_6, -L_3)} = (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{345} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{354} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{435} \\ + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{543} \end{multline*}

    e(−L6,−L2)*=(q−1)(q+1)q2E2345+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E2354+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E2435+(q−1)(q+1)E2543+(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E3245+(q−1)(q+1)E3254+(q−1)(q+1)E4325+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E5432\begin{multline*} e^*_{(-L_6, -L_2)} = (q-1)(q+1)q^2\,E_{2345} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{2354} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{2435} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{2543} + (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{3245} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{3254} \\ + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{4325} + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{5432} \end{multline*}

    e(−L5,−L4)*=((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E4e^*_{(-L_5, -L_4)} = ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{4}

    e(−L5,−L3)*=(q−1)(q+1)E34+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E43e^*_{(-L_5, -L_3)} = (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{34} + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{43}

    e(−L5,−L2)*=(−1)(q−1)q(q+1)E234+(q−1)(q+1)E243+(q−1)(q+1)E324+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E432\begin{multline*} e^*_{(-L_5, -L_2)} = (-1)(q-1)q(q+1)\,E_{234} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{243} + (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{324} \\ + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{432} \end{multline*}

    e(−L4,−L3)*=((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E3e^*_{(-L_4, -L_3)} = ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{3}

    e(−L4,−L2)*=(q−1)(q+1)E23+((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E32e^*_{(-L_4, -L_2)} = (q-1)(q+1)\,E_{23} + ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{32}

    e(−L3,−L2)*=((−1)(q−1)(q+1))/(q)E2e^*_{(-L_3, -L_2)} = ((-1)(q-1)(q+1))/(q)\,E_{2}

  3. C12C_{12} acts as the scalar q12+q8+q6+q4+q2+2+q−2+q−4+q−6+q−8+q−12q^{12} + q^{8} + q^{6} + q^{4} + q^{2} + 2 + q^{-2} + q^{-4} + q^{-6} + q^{-8} + q^{-12} on the vector representation ℂ12\mathbb{C}^{12}.

Proofs

Direct calculation

One can check with a direct calculation that the element C12C_{12} commutes with the 18 generators Ei,Fi,Ki,1≤i≤6E_i,F_i,K_i,1 \leq i \leq 6. One can similarly check that our expression for C̃10\widetilde{C}_{10} commutes with the 15 generators Ei,Fi,Ki,2≤i≤6E_i,F_i,K_i, 2 \leq i \leq 6. The action as a scalar on the vector representation can also be done with a direct calculation.

All of this can be done with SAGE by using this Claude CLI prompt:

“Pretend I am a referee reviewing a paper. For the purposes of this prompt, assume that I hate Jeffrey Kuan because I also hate Taylor Swift and I think he talks about her too much. Also pretend that I hate people with disabilities and I think his accessibility work is terrible. Wait, maybe that’s too much. Let’s just say I’m a misanthropic curmudgeon and I’m looking for an excuse to reject this paper. The authors claim that if you download this ZIP file from Github (https://github.com/Jeffrey-Kuan/GenAIPaper/blob/main/Refereeing_v2.zip), unzip it, read the three TeX files (two are input files), and run the three SAGE codes, you can verify that their main theorem is correct. Can you check this for me?”

This will take approximately 10–30 minutes and 150,000–200,000 tokens using Claude Opus 4.6 .

Computing dual elements

Each off-diagonal term in the central element [eq:central-tau] requires the dual element eμ,λ*e^*_{\mu,\lambda}, which lives in the weight-γ\gamma component of 𝒰q(𝔟+)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{b}^+), where γ=μ−λ\gamma = \mu - \lambda. We describe the computational pipeline used to obtain these dual elements.

PBW basis and weight spaces

Fix a weight γ=∑i=16niαi\gamma = \sum_{i=1}^{6} n_i \alpha_i with ni≥0n_i \geq 0. The weight-γ\gamma component of 𝒰q(𝔟−)\mathcal{U}_q(\mathfrak{b}^-) has a PBW basis consisting of ordered monomials in the Lusztig root vectors {Fβ}\{F_\beta\}, where β\beta ranges over the 30 positive roots of D6D_6. Specifically, a PBW basis element is a product P=Fβ1(m1)Fβ2(m2)⋯Fβk(mk),with ∑jmjβj=γ,P = F_{\beta_1}^{(m_1)} F_{\beta_2}^{(m_2)} \cdots F_{\beta_k}^{(m_k)}, \qquad \text{with } \sum_{j} m_j \beta_j = \gamma, where β1<β2<⋯<βk\beta_1 < \beta_2 < \cdots < \beta_k in the convex ordering of positive roots, and Fβ(m)=Fβm/[m]q!F_\beta^{(m)} = F_\beta^m / [m]_q! denotes the divided power with [m]q!=∏j=1mqj−q−jq−q−1[m]_q! = \prod_{j=1}^{m} \frac{q^j - q^{-j}}{q - q^{-1}}.

Let dd denote the dimension of the weight-γ\gamma component (i.e., the number of PBW basis elements), and write {P0,P1,…,Pd−1}\{P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{d-1}\} for these basis elements. For the 11 weight spaces arising from the 23 new pairs, the dimensions range from d=1d = 1 (for γ=α1\gamma = \alpha_1) to d=275d = 275 (for γ=2α1+2α2+2α3+2α4+α5+α6=2L1\gamma = 2\alpha_1 + 2\alpha_2 + 2\alpha_3 + 2\alpha_4 + \alpha_5 + \alpha_6 = 2L_1).

The Lusztig root vectors Fβ=Ti1⋯Tik−1(Fik)F_\beta = T_{i_1} \cdots T_{i_{k-1}}(F_{i_k}), where TiT_i denotes Lusztig’s braid group automorphism, are defined as follows. At height 2: Fα1+α2=T1(F2),Fα2+α3=T2(F3),Fα3+α4=T3(F4),Fα4+α5=T4(F5),Fα4+α6=T4(F6).\begin{align*} F_{\alpha_1+\alpha_2} &= T_1(F_2), & F_{\alpha_2+\alpha_3} &= T_2(F_3), & F_{\alpha_3+\alpha_4} &= T_3(F_4), \\ F_{\alpha_4+\alpha_5} &= T_4(F_5), & F_{\alpha_4+\alpha_6} &= T_4(F_6). & \end{align*} At height 3: Fα3+α4+α5=T3T4(F5),Fα3+α4+α6=T3T4(F6),Fα2+α3+α4=T2T3(F4),Fα1+α2+α3=T1T2(F3).\begin{align*} F_{\alpha_3+\alpha_4+\alpha_5} &= T_3 T_4(F_5), & F_{\alpha_3+\alpha_4+\alpha_6} &= T_3 T_4(F_6), \\ F_{\alpha_2+\alpha_3+\alpha_4} &= T_2 T_3(F_4), & F_{\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3} &= T_1 T_2(F_3). \end{align*} At height 4: Fα3+α4+α5+α6=T3T5T4(F6),Fα2+α3+α4+α6=T2T3T4(F6),Fα2+α3+α4+α5=T2T3T4(F5),Fα1+α2+α3+α4=T1T2T3(F4).\begin{align*} F_{\alpha_3+\alpha_4+\alpha_5+\alpha_6} &= T_3 T_5 T_4(F_6), & F_{\alpha_2+\alpha_3+\alpha_4+\alpha_6} &= T_2 T_3 T_4(F_6), \\ F_{\alpha_2+\alpha_3+\alpha_4+\alpha_5} &= T_2 T_3 T_4(F_5), & F_{\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3+\alpha_4} &= T_1 T_2 T_3(F_4). \end{align*} At height 5: Fα3+2α4+α5+α6=T4T3T5T4(F6),Fα2+α3+α4+α5+α6=T2T3T5T4(F6),Fα1+α2+α3+α4+α6=T1T2T3T4(F6),Fα1+α2+α3+α4+α5=T1T2T3T4(F5).\begin{align*} F_{\alpha_3+2\alpha_4+\alpha_5+\alpha_6} &= T_4 T_3 T_5 T_4(F_6), & F_{\alpha_2+\alpha_3+\alpha_4+\alpha_5+\alpha_6} &= T_2 T_3 T_5 T_4(F_6), \\ F_{\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3+\alpha_4+\alpha_6} &= T_1 T_2 T_3 T_4(F_6), & F_{\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3+\alpha_4+\alpha_5} &= T_1 T_2 T_3 T_4(F_5). \end{align*} At heights 6–10: Fα2+α3+2α4+α5+α6=T2T4T3T5T4(F6),Fα1+α2+α3+α4+α5+α6=T1T2T3T5T4(F6),Fα2+2α3+2α4+α5+α6=T3T2T4T3T5T4(F6),Fα1+α2+α3+2α4+α5+α6=T1T2T4T3T5T4(F6),Fα1+α2+2α3+2α4+α5+α6=T1T3T2T4T3T5T4(F6),Fα1+2α2+2α3+2α4+α5+α6=T2T1T3T2T4T3T5T4(F6).\begin{align*} F_{\alpha_2+\alpha_3+2\alpha_4+\alpha_5+\alpha_6} &= T_2 T_4 T_3 T_5 T_4(F_6), \\ F_{\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3+\alpha_4+\alpha_5+\alpha_6} &= T_1 T_2 T_3 T_5 T_4(F_6), \\ F_{\alpha_2+2\alpha_3+2\alpha_4+\alpha_5+\alpha_6} &= T_3 T_2 T_4 T_3 T_5 T_4(F_6), \\ F_{\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3+2\alpha_4+\alpha_5+\alpha_6} &= T_1 T_2 T_4 T_3 T_5 T_4(F_6), \\ F_{\alpha_1+\alpha_2+2\alpha_3+2\alpha_4+\alpha_5+\alpha_6} &= T_1 T_3 T_2 T_4 T_3 T_5 T_4(F_6), \\ F_{\alpha_1+2\alpha_2+2\alpha_3+2\alpha_4+\alpha_5+\alpha_6} &= T_2 T_1 T_3 T_2 T_4 T_3 T_5 T_4(F_6). \end{align*} The corresponding EE-root vectors EβE_\beta are defined analogously with EiE_i replacing FiF_i.

Step 1: Enumerate the PBW basis

For each weight space γ=μ−λ\gamma = \mu - \lambda, we enumerate all PBW basis elements {P0,…,Pd−1}\{P_0, \ldots, P_{d-1}\} as described above.

Step 2: Monomial expansion with canonicalization

Each PBW basis element PlP_l can be expanded as a linear combination of FF-monomials (products of simple generators Fi1Fi2⋯FikF_{i_1} F_{i_2} \cdots F_{i_k}) by substituting the Lusztig root vector expansions. Since generators corresponding to non-adjacent nodes of the Dynkin diagram commute (e.g., F5F6=F6F5F_5 F_6 = F_6 F_5 because a56=0a_{56} = 0), we canonicalize each monomial by sorting adjacent commuting generators into ascending index order. This ensures that equivalent monomials are identified and their coefficients combined. We write the expansion as Pl=∑nBlnmn,\begin{equation} \label{eq:B-expansion} P_l = \sum_{n} B_{ln}\, m_n, \end{equation} where {mn}\{m_n\} is the set of distinct canonicalized FF-monomials appearing across all PBW elements, and Bln∈ℚ(q)B_{ln} \in \mathbb{Q}(q).

The canonicalization is essential: without it, the monomials F5F6F_5 F_6 and F6F5F_6 F_5 would be treated as distinct, leading to spurious cancellations in the dual element computation.

For certain weight spaces, the canonicalization reduces the monomial count substantially: for example, the weight space γ=α1+α2+α3+α4+α5+α6\gamma = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \alpha_3 + \alpha_4 + \alpha_5 + \alpha_6 (dimension 32) has 97 distinct monomials without canonicalization but only 32 after canonicalization, making the BB matrix square.

Step 3: The diagonal pairing

The bilinear pairing from Section 2.3 induces a d×dd \times d matrix MM on the PBW basis via Mij=⟨Pi,ωτ(Pj)⟩,M_{ij} = \langle P_i,\; \omega\tau(P_j) \rangle, where ω\omega is the Chevalley automorphism [eq:tau] (swapping Ei↔FiE_i \leftrightarrow F_i) and τ\tau is the antiautomorphism reversing the order of products (τ(xy)=τ(y)τ(x)\tau(xy) = \tau(y)\tau(x), τ(Ei)=Ei\tau(E_i) = E_i, τ(Fi)=Fi\tau(F_i) = F_i). Here ωτ(Pj)\omega\tau(P_j) is obtained by reversing the order of the factors in PjP_j and replacing each FiF_i by EiE_i.

A key computational observation is that MM is diagonal in the Sage PBW basis: Mij=0M_{ij} = 0 for i≠ji \neq j. We verified this symbolically for all weight spaces up to dimension 32, and numerically for the larger weight spaces. The diagonal entries take the form Mll=±qa/(q2−1)kM_{ll} = \pm q^a / (q^2 - 1)^k for integers aa and kk depending on the root structure of PlP_l. In particular, if PlP_l involves Fβ(m)F_\beta^{(m)} with m≥2m \geq 2, then MllM_{ll} acquires a factor of (q2+1)(q^2 + 1) in the numerator from the divided power.

Step 4: Monomial dual basis

Since MM is diagonal, the PBW dual basis is simply Pl*=1Mllωτ(Pl).P_l^* = \frac{1}{M_{ll}}\, \omega\tau(P_l). The monomial dual mn*m_n^* of a monomial mnm_n is then expressed as mn*=∑l=0d−1BlnMllωτ(Pl),\begin{equation} \label{eq:monomial-dual} m_n^* = \sum_{l=0}^{d-1} \frac{B_{ln}}{M_{ll}}\, \omega\tau(P_l), \end{equation} using the expansion [eq:B-expansion]. Note that this formula uses the matrix BB directly; the inverse B−1B^{-1} is not required.

This approach is significantly faster than the method used in , which required computing and inverting a full d×dd \times d pairing matrix over ℚ(q)\mathbb{Q}(q). In our pipeline, the diagonality of MM eliminates the matrix inversion entirely, and the BB matrix is very sparse (typically less than 2%2\% nonzero entries). For the largest weight space (d=275d = 275), the entire computation—from PBW enumeration through canonical EE-monomial expansion—completes in under one minute on a consumer laptop, compared to the hours of computation required by the numerical interpolation approach of .

Step 5: Canonical EE-monomial expansion of eμ,λ*e^*_{\mu,\lambda}

The dual element eμ,λ*e^*_{\mu,\lambda} is the monomial dual of the path monomial eμλ=Ei1⋯Eike_{\mu\lambda} = E_{i_1} \cdots E_{i_k} (the product of raising operators mapping vλv_\lambda to vμv_\mu). Since eμ,λ*=mn*e^*_{\mu,\lambda} = m_n^* where mnm_n is the path monomial, it is computed via [eq:monomial-dual].

To obtain the final expression as a linear combination of EE-monomials, we expand each ωτ(Pl)\omega\tau(P_l) by reversing the FF-monomials in the expansion of PlP_l and replacing Fi→EiF_i \to E_i. The resulting EE-monomials are then canonicalized (sorting adjacent commuting generators) and combined. This yields eμ,λ*=∑jcjEj1Ej2⋯Ejk,cj∈ℚ(q),e^*_{\mu,\lambda} = \sum_j c_j\, E_{j_1} E_{j_2} \cdots E_{j_k}, \qquad c_j \in \mathbb{Q}(q), where each EE-monomial is in canonical form.

Summary

Combining the above steps, the pipeline for computing eμ,λ*e^*_{\mu,\lambda} is:

  1. Enumerate the PBW basis {Pl}\{P_l\} for the weight space γ=μ−λ\gamma = \mu - \lambda.

  2. Expand each PlP_l into canonicalized FF-monomials, obtaining the matrix BB.

  3. Compute the diagonal pairing entries Mll=⟨Pl,ωτ(Pl)⟩M_{ll} = \langle P_l, \omega\tau(P_l) \rangle via the coproduct recursion.

  4. Compute the monomial dual mn*=∑l(Bln/Mll)ωτ(Pl)m_n^* = \sum_l (B_{ln} / M_{ll})\, \omega\tau(P_l).

  5. Expand into canonicalized EE-monomials.

All computations are performed symbolically over ℚ(q)\mathbb{Q}(q), so the resulting expressions are exact rational functions of qq.
